Explaining architecture of VERCEL !
So I deployed my worker on Railway but had to use cron jobs. I explained it in a few posts back, but learning not to deploy that worker on Vercel was the thing that made me research how Vercel actually works, because Vercel is the go-to deployment platform for any dev, right? So this post will talk about it:
1. What is actually inside your Vercel deployment? And why it’s not a server.
2. What service does it use?
3. Why can’t you deploy workers on Vercel, and when should you deploy on Railway instead?
So Vercel is serverless, meaning you don’t have to handle the server on your own; they do it for you.
The thing to note is that Vercel does not keep running your app forever. Your app is not running most of the time and is asleep. So it’s not actually a server.
But the interesting thing is that when a request hits your backend route, it spins up an AWS Lambda function. That means only that API route activates, not the entire app. That AWS Lambda spins up that route (function), gets a request, returns a response, and kills it instantly.
So a Vercel deployment waits for a request -> spins up a Lambda function -> returns a response -> kills it.
In the case of a queue, the worker has to keep listening to that queue, which is something like // while(true) keep listening to that queue //.
So in this case, no request comes to your deployment, which is the sole reason for Vercel to activate, hence it won’t.
Also, Vercel times out if a function keeps running, hence killing it without returning a response. This means long tasks that may take 2–3 minutes can’t be done on Vercel.
Let’s talk about how Vercel serves the frontend. When you deploy on Vercel, it builds your code and caches it on a CDN (Content Delivery Network). The frontend is then served from there to your browser, and your browser runs the code.
So this was the overall architecture that you should know.
Thank you.
